text: I usually have toast with scrambled eggs and a cup of tea. It's quite simple, really. Sometimes my granddaughter will come round and we'll make some pancakes together, which is lovely. If I'm feeling fancy, I might whip out some fresh fruit from our little garden – strawberries, blueberries, raspberries... those sorts always put me in a cheerful mood. Other than that, a bit of toast and eggs gets me going for the morning.

My usual lunchtime is a bit chaotic, I'm afraid! Since my son still lives nearby, he likes to drop me off some leftovers from his place every now and then. It saves him the trouble of cooking and makes our catch-ups more pleasant. If he's not available, I usually heat something simple like soup or pasta, maybe make myself a sandwich. Sometimes I'll pop out to the café near here – they have lovely vegetable soups and crusty bread; I find it nice to take a break amidst all these books and papers scattered everywhere. Afternoons tend to be a bit sluggish, so even a cuppa and a slice of cake helps perk me up before heading back home.
I try to be in bed by 10 o'clock, preferably a bit sooner if I'm feeling quite tired. It helps me get a good night's sleep so I feel refreshed for the morning.

I think a gentle morning routine helps me feel refreshed and ready to tackle the day. It's all about starting slowly – perhaps having a cuppa tea, enjoying some fresh air on my front porch while checking the weather forecast, maybe even jotting down a quick note in my gratitude journal before breakfast. A bit of exercise, like going for a brisk walk, is essential too; it gets those endorphins flowing and clears the mind. After that, I find I'm more focused and prepared for whatever comes my way. Of course, there's always room for adjustments based on how I'm feeling each day!

I think networking is essential in our line of work, where relationships between colleagues and departments matter a great deal. Over the past twenty-five years working here, I made sure to build strong connections with people across various departments – we'd attend team meetings, collaborate on projects, and simply chat informally during breaks. My door was always open, so anyone could come to me for advice or guidance whenever needed. It helped that my manager encouraged us to be proactive about building these networks; there were regular seminars, workshops, and training sessions too. In more recent years since retirement, while I'm not actively involved in professional organizations anymore, I still try to nurture those friendships and acquaintances developed during my employment. We have an annual reunion event at the local community center which has been wonderful – catching up with former colleagues who now live nearby is lovely. It really comes down to genuine interest in others and being willing to lend a hand or listen when required. Simple human connection goes a long way!

I think what helps me most is having a dedicated workspace set up before starting work. It makes everything feel more professional and sets a boundary between my personal space and work time. A nice cuppa always seems to help too! And of course, sticking to regular working hours really does make all the difference – for me, those are usually between 9 am and noon. Trying to avoid distractions during that time allows me to focus quite effectively. Occasionally, taking a walk outside during my break will refresh me nicely. Overall, being mindful of how much time I spend checking emails and messages throughout the day has helped me manage interruptions better. Simple routines like these little habits seem to give me an edge while working remotely.

I'm getting more used to them now, but I must admit, I find it a bit tricky sometimes. My grandsons have been teaching me how to use my iPad, which is lovely, but it still feels like I'm fumbling around a bit. They're very patient with me, bless them! When we do our regular video call, I make sure to sit somewhere with decent lighting and try to relax – although I do wish there was an easier way to adjust the brightness and volume. Still, it's great catching up with them; it makes me feel so much closer despite being apart.

I must admit, getting used to being out of an office environment has taken some adjusting, but I've found a rhythm that works for me. You know, people say retirement is all about freedom, but sometimes it feels like losing direction! For me, staying motivated comes from setting personal targets and routines. Every morning, I make sure to have a plan – whether it's tackling my gardening, working on a puzzle, or simply enjoying a nice cuppa while catching up on current events. It's funny how much discipline we need even in our free time. Having a daily "to-do" list helps me prioritize what I want to accomplish. Of course, flexibility is key; if I'm feeling tired or uninspired, I take a break and come back later. My grandkids always tease me about still having a strict morning routine – but hey, someone has to keep them grounded! Another thing that really motivates me is spending time outdoors. Being surrounded by nature makes everything feel more manageable, don't you think? And, let's be honest, there's nothing quite like the satisfaction of watching something you planted grow into something beautiful – it puts things into perspective, doesn't it? Lastly, making time for those little pleasures in life keeps me going too. Whether it

I think one has to be flexible, really. As a former admin assistant, I was used to having a set routine and knowing exactly what needed doing each day. But when working in different environments like schools and surgeries, you have to adapt to whatever comes your way. It might mean juggling multiple projects simultaneously or prioritising tasks based on urgency rather than preference. You need to remain focused and make decisions about which task is most critical first. For me, being organised helps. Keeping an overview of everything going on and breaking tasks into manageable chunks makes it easier to adjust plans accordingly. And of course, communicating clearly with colleagues ensures everyone knows where we stand so we're all pulling in the same direction. A bit of common sense and a willingness to roll with the punches – it's all part of being able to navigate those changes effectively!

Advocating for myself is quite simple, really. When needed, I speak clearly and assertively, explaining my thoughts and feelings. I'm a straight-shooter, so I try to be honest while still being polite and considerate of others. If there's an issue or concern, I'll raise it calmly and respectfully, usually during one-to-one meetings or informal chats rather than making a scene. My style might seem too direct to some, but I believe that getting things done efficiently requires speaking your mind – as long as you're prepared to listen to opposing views afterwards!

When I feel like I'm being misunderstood, I try to take a step back and consider what might be going on. Sometimes, I'll ask someone directly how my words were received, and more often than not, it's been misinterpreted due to tone of voice or something else entirely. Other times, I find myself explaining myself again and hoping the other person will listen properly this time. It's funny, isn't it, how sometimes we think others have understood us perfectly fine while really, there's still some misunderstanding lingering beneath the surface? I've come to accept that clarity is key; speaking clearly and using simple language helps me avoid confusion. If all else fails, rephrasing what was said may resolve issues, though it does require patience from everyone involved! Ultimately, knowing exactly why people react the way they do has helped me adjust my behaviour accordingly – it makes me a better communicator, don't you think?

I love spending time with my grandkids! When we have a gathering together, I usually try to make sure everyone is having fun and comfortable. We'll often play games like Monopoly or Scrabble, or watch a movie together. My grandson has taken quite an interest in cooking lately, so sometimes he helps me bake something special, which always brings out his creativity. After dinner, we might go for a walk around the block or sit by the fireplace and chat about our daily lives. It's lovely to catch up and hear all about what's been happening since the last visit. As much as I'd like more one-on-one time with each child now, with four young ones running around, those moments are precious though! We always end with lots of hugs, laughter, and reminiscing – even sharing some funny anecdotes about when your grandfather was still alive... He loved being surrounded by such lively company!

To be honest, I'm not too keen on those platforms myself. As someone who was more focused on my work during her working years rather than building a personal brand, I never really felt the need to have one. Now that I'm retired, I'd much prefer to spend my time engaging with family and friends in meaningful conversations rather than staring at screens all day. However, I am aware that many people find them helpful for staying connected professionally so, I suppose having a simple presence there is fine. But I wouldn't say I'll be spending any great deal of time on it!

I think sensitivity is all about approaching a situation with respect and genuine interest. If someone shares something personal or vulnerable with me, I try to listen attentively and show that I'm really paying attention. Sometimes that might mean pausing before responding so I can gather my thoughts or ask clarifying questions to make sure I understand what they're getting across. If needed, I'll acknowledge how they feel and validate those emotions – you know, letting them know that their feelings are normal and okay. It's essential to remember everyone has different comfort levels when discussing sensitive subjects; some need space and time to process while others may appreciate open discussion straight away. My aim is always to be honest yet compassionate, ensuring the conversation remains constructive rather than confrontational. It's quite simple, isn't it? Treat others as we'd like to be treated ourselves!

Well, I'm quite direct, so I suppose one could say I'm rather resistant to peer pressure. If someone tries to persuade me into something I don't feel is necessary or sensible, I'll usually explain my reasons clearly and politely decline. It's always best to be honest and transparent, isn't it? Of course, there have been instances where I'd yield eventually, especially among close friends who know how stubborn I am! But generally speaking, I prefer to think things through myself before making a decision, rather than being swayed by what others might consider popular opinion.

I try to be quite cautious about those targeted ads, to be honest. They can be a bit unsettling knowing exactly how much information is being collected and used. While I'm aware some of them might be harmless, there's still something unnerving about having my personal preferences tailored so precisely. Still, I suppose I have little choice nowadays; I mean, everyone does seem to accept these sorts of practices now. But I find myself opting out whenever I possibly can – switching off cookies, using private browsing modes... It all feels like an invasion of one's personal space, even though I know intellectually I should probably trust the companies involved.

Well, I must admit, managing multiple tabs is a bit of a struggle for me. To be honest, I find myself getting flustered and feeling like my mind gets divided between too many open windows. Sometimes I'll close them one by one until only two or three remain, hoping that helps me focus better. But sometimes I feel like there's so much clutter out there, and it's hard to sift through all the information without feeling overwhelmed. My grandson does try to help me with his tech expertise, which is lovely – he patiently explains how to tidy everything up! Still, it's something I'm working on improving, though I'd rather stick to what I know best: writing things down in notebooks or using paper files. Old habits die hard, I suppose!

To be honest, I find those sorts of people quite infuriating! When faced with a trollish comment, my first instinct is usually to ignore them altogether – why feed into someone else's negativity? However, I do think there have been instances where responding politely but firmly has helped to de-escalate the situation. A simple "I'm sorry you feel strongly enough to post such a comment" might suffice. But let me tell you, some individuals seem to take great pleasure in stirring up trouble, so sometimes discretion is key! My grandfather used to say, "If you rise above, they'll come crawling." He was always saying stuff like that, but his words still ring true today. I try to remember that most people aren't worth engaging with unless it's absolutely necessary. As he'd say, "A gentle breeze will blow away the weeds; don't waste your breath on them!"

When there's a problem with our video call, my grandsons always jump into action! They're very helpful like that. One will usually try to troubleshoot the issue while the other one holds the camera so we can still see each other. If that doesn't work, I'll ask them to bring me another device or find someone else who might know what's going wrong. Sometimes, it takes some trial and error, but eventually, we manage to sort out the problem. It's lovely having such tech-savvy young adults looking out for us, isn't it?

I find a simple system works best for me. Every month, I sit down with my calendar and list out all the due dates for each bill – including council tax, water rates, insurance, and so forth. Then, using my trusty planner, I break them down into manageable chunks and make sure to set reminders weeks before the payment is actually due. If there's any uncertainty about amounts or frequencies, I double-check with the relevant organisations directly to avoid missing payments. It may seem old-fashioned, but pen and paper still work wonders for keeping track of finances! A bit of planning ahead ensures everything gets done smoothly, and I never have to worry too much about forgetting deadlines. Plus, I like having those numbers add up neatly across my spreadsheets afterwards...

I try to be quite ruthless about cancelling any subscription that we haven't used in a while. If something is taking money out of my account every month and hasn't been of much use since last autumn, I'll probably cut ties with it. But if there's one I know will come in handy later, like our local history magazine, I might consider keeping it. Really, though, most of my decision-making comes down to whether or not I think I could really miss what it offers – or whether it'd cause more hassle than it's worth to stick with it.

I'd always say wait until absolutely necessary before buying something major like a new washing machine. My late husband was quite keen on getting everything brand-new straight away, but he never really lived past his mid-seventies anyway. As for me now, I think I'll replace my current one once we're closer to selling our place – likely next spring, perhaps with some savings set aside first. It might be nice to have all these gadgets nowadays, but waste is so much more expensive! We should make sure we cover every expense properly.

I'm a bit old-fashioned in my way of thinking, I suppose. When faced with deciding whether to buy something new or second-hand, I always think about what will last me longer and fit into our little household comfortably. If it's something like books or furniture, I usually opt for a gently-used option – there's so much wonderful stuff out there being given away or sold cheaply, isn't there? If it's an appliance or electronics, though, I tend to be more pragmatic. I'll consider how long we're likely to need it, its condition, and any reviews or recommendations from trusted sources before making a decision. We already have some lovely vintage items in the house, and while they may require extra TLC, I find that giving them a new lease on life is rewarding. But honestly, these days, I rely quite heavily on my son to advise me on tech purchases! He knows his stuff far better than I do.

I think my 'waste not, want not' upbringing has definitely helped me develop a habit of being mindful of spending and avoiding unnecessary expenses. As soon as we were kids, Mum always taught us to be frugal and make our money stretch. It stuck, really. Even now, I try to live below my means and save whenever possible. When I'm out shopping, I look for bargains and shop during sales. We never went into debt while we had a mortgage, so I know how to manage finances effectively. And, of course, having been a woman who's lost loved ones financially, like my husband, I find it essential to prioritize saving and securing our legacy for future generations.

I make sure to plan ahead during those periods. I check my list, prioritise what we really need and try to catch any bargains that fit our budget. It's always a bit chaotic, but I find it's worth it to snag some decent deals on essentials like winter clothing and bedding. And, of course, I love getting excited about summer holidays – I try to sneak in some bargain-hunting while keeping an eye out for discounts on books for the grandkids. My son usually helps me navigate the supermarkets now; he knows all the best times to go and how much money I should spend!

Portion control is a bit tricky for me, I must admit. Growing up, my mum always said "use what you need" and we'd have seconds only if we really wanted them. But now, since being widowed, I find myself reaching for second portions more often because there isn't anyone else to share with! *laughs* My grandkids will tease me about eating too much though! I try to gauge how hungry I am before serving food, and stop once I feel satisfied rather than stuffed. It's all about balance, you know? A nice slice of cake or a handful of crisps here and there. Of course, I still indulge every now and then – what's life without a treat, eh?! Still, moderation seems key. Would love some advice from someone younger than me, perhaps...

I think I'd find a therapist who is warm and empathetic, someone who listens attentively and understands the importance of taking one's time to reflect on thoughts and feelings. A gentle, non-judgmental space where I feel safe sharing my concerns and working through issues. It might take some getting used to being the focus of conversation rather than always being focused on others, like I was during my teaching days, but I believe that's necessary for growth. Maybe something along those lines – nothing too intense or confrontational, please!

Preventing burnout is essential, isn't it? For me, it's all about finding a balance between being productive and taking breaks throughout the day. As a morning lark, my peak productivity hours are usually between 8 am and noon, so I try to tackle those focused periods first before stepping away to recharge. I make sure to take short breaks every hour or two – maybe grab a cuppa, stretch, or simply sit outside for some fresh air. It really helps to refresh the mind and come back feeling revitalised. In the evenings, I like to unwind with a gentle activity, such as reading or tending to my garden; these quiet moments allow me to relax and prepare for bed without any stimulating thoughts racing round my head. It's also crucial to set realistic expectations and priorities, ensuring I'm focusing on what truly matters most. I remind myself that saying "no" sometimes is perfectly fine – prioritising self-care and avoiding overcommitting can be liberating! A bit of downtime never hurts either; whether it's a Sunday afternoon spent lounging or an extra-long walk during the week, allowing oneself to disengage occasionally prevents feelings of exhaustion building up. Oh, and staying hydrated and eating nutritious food plays its part too! Taking care of one

I think a crucial part of getting better rest is establishing a calming pre-sleep routine, such as taking some warm tea, perhaps having a gentle bath before bed, and avoiding electronic devices an hour beforehand – all those blue lights interfere so much! And keeping my room nice and dark, too. You know, creating a peaceful atmosphere really helps me unwind. A bit of deep breathing exercises has been helpful, as well; reminds myself it's okay to let go of any racing thoughts. Getting into bed at 10 o'clock sharp ensures I'm asleep fairly soon, usually. Of course, there are dips mid-day, post-lunch slump being one of them, but generally speaking, I try to stick to a regular bedtime and rise-by-and-go routine. It makes sense, I suppose, why consistency is key.

I try to mix it up quite a bit, really. During the week, I usually stick to brisk walks along our local park paths during my morning break, which is lovely because I'm an early riser anyway. On Thursdays, though, I like to join forces with some of my neighbours who share similar fitness goals – we take turns having a gentle jog together while chatting. It helps make the time fly! On Fridays, since the weather tends to improve, I might treat myself to a longer walk into the nearby woods near here; there's something so calming about breathing in all those trees. Weekends are perfect for tending to my little vegetable patch in the backyard too, getting out among the soil and watching everything sprout... nothing beats being outdoors! It's nice to have these different routines to look forward to each day, rather than feeling stuck in one pattern all the time. Plus, staying active makes me feel more energised overall, even on those dips mid-day.

I tend to think very methodically about each option, weighing the pros and cons, considering my priorities and what will work best for me. It helps to break everything down into smaller parts and look at them individually rather than getting overwhelmed by a big decision. Sometimes having a cuppa while sitting quietly gives me space to reflect too! If someone explains something clearly and shows how their idea addresses specific issues, I'm more likely to be convinced. But I like to make sure there isn't an obvious alternative that might have been overlooked – that's why I appreciate straight talking and honest discussion. Ultimately, I try to find common ground between reason and intuition; sometimes one outweighs the other, but usually we end up finding a balance.

I think incorporating feedback is a crucial aspect of any solution. When receiving constructive comments, I like to take a moment to consider them thoughtfully before responding. It helps me put myself in others' shoes and try to identify areas where we might have gone astray. After digesting what's been said, I usually re-evaluate my initial idea or plan, making adjustments based on those insights. Sometimes, taking an extra step back allows me to look at the bigger picture – which may reveal alternative approaches that could be more effective. By being open to different viewpoints and actively considering how others perceive our work, I aim to refine and improve the outcome even further. For instance, during my time as a secretary, colleagues occasionally provided valuable suggestions on improving workflow processes. One such comment led us to adopt a fresh system for prioritising tasks, greatly boosting productivity. In many cases, though, I prefer to engage directly with individuals who offer feedback; sometimes simply discussing concerns verbally helps clarify issues better. Overall, incorporating thoughtful consideration of feedback enables developing strong working relationships built upon mutual respect and trust.

I'd say my approach is quite methodical. I like breaking down complex issues into smaller parts, looking at each element individually before piecing them together to form a complete picture. It helps me avoid getting lost in the bigger scheme of things and ensures I'm considering all relevant factors. Plus, being a bit systematic makes sure everything gets addressed rather than leaving any stone unturned – which has served me fairly well throughout my working life in admin roles. Of course, sometimes I wish there was more data to work with; clarity really does make all the difference!

I find that breaking things down into smaller steps helps me grasp them better. When faced with a complex issue or task, I try to identify what specific information is needed, make a list, and prioritize those items one by one. Sometimes, writing everything out by hand really slows me down enough so my brain has time to think more clearly before making decisions. It's almost like giving myself permission to take a bit longer to figure something out – sometimes that extra time allows me to notice connections between seemingly unrelated pieces of information which clarifies things quite nicely.

I think I'd say one area where I'm always looking to improve is my digital literacy. As much as I love staying connected with my loved ones and keeping myself informed, I find all these new technologies can be a bit overwhelming. I know I should probably sort out updating everything properly, but it feels like there's so many options! Sometimes I rely too heavily on my son for help, which isn't ideal. Perhaps setting aside some dedicated time each month to brush up on those basics will really make me feel more secure. Would be lovely to have someone break it down into manageable chunks – that way, I'll actually remember what I need to do instead of getting frustrated halfway through. Also, being a morning lark, I sometimes wish I could tap into that extra energy boost longer; maybe having smaller, achievable targets throughout the day might give me an edge. Getting enough sleep seems key though, and since I tend to wind down before bed, hopefully, that helps regulate my internal clock better. Just feeling generally content and satisfied with who I am has helped reduce any unnecessary worries I may have had previously. What's most valuable now is focusing on enjoying everyday moments rather than stressing over minor issues. Life's still full of surprises, but knowing how to navigate them makes

I think my approach has evolved a bit over the years. As someone who values tradition and familiarity, I have to admit that I was always a bit hesitant to try new things. But, having had some wonderful life experiences and being fortunate enough to be able to take calculated risks – like downsizing our home and moving closer to grandkids – has taught me that sometimes stepping out of one's comfort zone is necessary for growth. When faced with something new, I try to ask myself why it might be worth exploring. What could I gain from it? Is there an opportunity here to learn something valuable? Or will it genuinely improve my circumstances? It helps to break it down into smaller steps too; rather than feeling overwhelmed by the whole situation, breaking it down makes it feel more manageable. And of course, having support from loved ones, whether it's a partner, children, or close friends, really does make all the difference. Ultimately, I suppose what works best for me is taking my time, weighing everything up, and making informed decisions based on logic rather than emotion. It's never easy, but that way I avoid unnecessary worries and focus on finding solutions that work best for me.

I make sure to carve out a bit each morning before the world gets too busy. It's my favourite part of the day, really – there's something so peaceful about sipping tea while getting lost in a good book. My grandkids might even join me occasionally, which is lovely. We'll sit together, turn pages, and chat about what we're reading. It's a wonderful way to spend time together. When they're away at school, though, I try to squeeze in some dedicated reading time myself, usually first thing in the morning until lunchtime. Some people think I'm crazy, saying I have an hour to myself every day, but it's essential to recharge, isn't it?

I think it's all about word-of-mouth and recommendations! My husband used to read a lot before he...well, you know. He was always recommending books to me, and his suggestions usually turned out to be quite enjoyable. Since he's been gone, my son has taken over, really getting into writing some fantastic historical novels, which have introduced me to many wonderful authors like Hilary Mantel and Ken Follett. But even more recently, I met someone at our local literary society who told us about an author named Philippa Gregory – her Thomas Cromwell series is absolutely fascinating. We got hooked, so now we're following along together! Sometimes browsing the shelves at the library or second-hand bookshops will lead me to something entirely different; perhaps a beautiful cover catches my eye or I stumble upon a recommendation poster. And sometimes, being part of these little groups allows people to share what they've enjoyed lately, which helps spark interest in me too! It seems there's nothing better than discovering something completely new and falling in love with it – whether it's a genre or an author.

I find myself humming along quietly while sewing, knitting, or working on a puzzle – usually something gentle and soothing to let my mind wander. Occasionally, I might put on some classical music and listen while tidying up the living room before settling into a nice cuppa tea. Nothing too energetic, really; I like to unwind rather than energise!

I must admit, I'm a bit old-fashioned, so while I appreciate the convenience of having something to listen to during commutes or chores, I haven't really taken to audiobooks or podcasts like some people do. However, my granddaughter has been encouraging me to give them another try, so I recently downloaded an app on her suggestion and started listening to a historical biography of Winston Churchill. It was quite engaging, actually! The narrator had done a lovely job bringing his story to life, and I found myself looking forward to our daily chats afterwards to discuss what he'd said. Now, I think I might be tempted to venture into other genres – perhaps some nature documentaries or gardening shows... who knows? But for now, I still prefer getting lost in a good novel or working on my own writing projects.

I thoroughly enjoy spending my free time tending to my vegetable garden. It's such a wonderful way to connect with nature and get some exercise while being outdoors. There's something very satisfying about watching those little seeds sprout into lovely plants. Plus, I find it quite therapeutic – all the digging and weeding helps me unwind. Sometimes I'll bake a cake using fresh fruits and herbs from my garden afterwards, which is always a treat! Occasionally, I might take a walk along the nearby canal path with my grandkids; they love spotting ducks and birds. All in all, keeping busy and staying active has become an essential part of my daily routine, really.

I must say, there's nothing quite like having some me-time to myself. You know, a cuppa tea, maybe some fresh air in my garden, tending to my vegetables... It's lovely to be able to work on my plants, watch them grow, and feel a sense of accomplishment knowing I'm providing something nourishing for my family. And of course, getting lost in a good book is always a treat – whether it's history, biography, or a bit of romance, as long as it's engaging, I'm happy! There's something so comforting about being surrounded by my own thoughts, enjoying the peace and quiet, and just recharging my batteries before tackling whatever comes next.

I love exploring different flavours! When trying a new cuisine, I tend to stick with dishes I know have all the essential ingredients – vegetables, meat, grains... You know, like a hearty stew or something simple yet satisfying. I find comfort food really helps me ease into unfamiliar tastes. My late husband used to say I was quite traditional in my eating habits, but he always encouraged me to venture out and try new things. Now, since his passing, I still follow similar patterns, though occasionally we'll go out to an international restaurant together before returning home to cook more British fare. Still, our grandkids love introducing us to modern twists on classic dishes, so there's some experimentation going on too! Would be lovely to take cooking classes sometime soon; I think I'd feel much braver about sampling foreign flavours then.

I think that's a great way to ask! You know, people often assume I'm quite predictable because I live alone, but I make an effort to mix things up. For instance, every other Sunday is dedicated to my grandkids – we have breakfast together, go out for walks, play games... It's lovely bonding time. In between those Sundays, I like to spend some time tending to my garden; there's something therapeutic about getting my hands dirty and watching seeds sprout into actual plants. And, of course, I always try to squeeze in some reading before bed, whether it's historical fiction or real-life accounts of remarkable women – history has such fascinating stories! When I need a break from all these structured activities, though, I'll pick up a puzzle or two – crosswords, perhaps, or one of those jigsaw puzzles where you assemble images. It's nice to unwind in front of the television too; our local station does an excellent job broadcasting nature documentaries, which never cease to amaze me. The thing is, having done various jobs throughout my working life, I find it rather satisfying to have different routines now that I'm retired. Keeps things fresh and exciting, you know? Now, how about you – what kind of diversions

I'm a bit of an old-fashioned one, I suppose. I like keeping a notebook for my hobbies, mainly because it helps me reflect on what went well and what didn't, and jot down any tips or ideas I might have for future attempts. For gardening, I find it quite satisfying to record the names of different plants we're growing, how much water and nutrients they need, and notes on pest control – all that sort of thing. And I must admit, writing down our grandkids' birthdays and milestones has become rather enjoyable too; it's lovely looking back on those memories later on. A simple pen-and-paper system works perfectly fine for me.

I'm quite particular about keeping my personal space tidy and organized. Since retiring, I've had more freedom to focus on what brings me joy – like tending to my garden! However, I still believe a sense of structure is essential to staying grounded and focused. For instance, every morning before starting work on any projects, I take some time to review my planner calendar, check emails, and make sure everything is running smoothly. My daily routines have become a bit flexible now, so I try to allocate specific times for tasks based on importance rather than strict schedules. In terms of paperwork, I rely heavily on digital tools whenever possible – I find they're efficient, yet easy to update. But there are certain documents, such as family records and recipes, where I prefer handwritten notes because I feel those hold sentimental value. A dedicated notebook helps keep them all together nicely. It might sound an unusual combination, but sometimes putting away one task allows another area of my life to flourish better; taking breaks and allowing myself time to unwind seems crucial too! Lastly, having regular tidying sessions has been helpful for maintaining mental clarity – there's something therapeutic about getting clutter out of sight. What works best for me could be different for others, though; everyone should experiment until finding methods that

I like to have a cuppa tea while getting ready, make myself some toast with fresh jam and butter – still got my recipe from my grandmother! Then I take a quick look out into the garden, check the weather forecast, and plan out what I'd like to accomplish during the day. After dressing properly, usually wear something sensible yet stylish, I grab my bag with all essentials like reading material, medication, and any other necessary documents. Sometimes I might jot down reminders or notes on my agenda to tackle later. Once I'm set, I'll catch a bus, which is very convenient since I only need public transport these days due to stopping driving three years ago. It gives me plenty of time to read the local paper, listen to the radio, or simply enjoy the scenery.

I try my best to be mindful of our energy consumption at home. We have double-glazed windows, which helps a lot during winter, and we insulate every room thoroughly. In summer, we open curtains and blinds to let sunlight in during the morning, then close them before the sun gets too strong. Our boiler is an efficient one, and we turn it off whenever possible – like when we're out, or overnight. My husband used to say we could save even more money by using draught excluders and thermal curtains, so now all rooms have those fitted. When guests come over, I remind everyone to switch lights off when leaving, which makes me feel rather strict! It may seem little, but these tiny habits add up. Plus, having some plants inside really does make a difference – they absorb carbon dioxide and release oxygen, so that's a bonus! I must admit, though, since retirement, there isn't much motivation left in terms of making changes... but sometimes I glance at my energy bills and think maybe it'd be worth looking into smart thermostats or something similar. Still, nothing major planned yet; simplicity suits us quite nicely, thank you very much!

I must admit, I'm a bit wary of all these new-fangled gadgets myself. To be honest, I find them quite overwhelming. My son tries to tell me how much easier they make our lives, but I'm not convinced. We have some simple ones like an Amazon Echo Dot in my living room – mainly because he insisted we had one! It plays radio stations and tells us the weather. But I only really notice its usefulness during those rare moments when I need to hear the latest news or music while I'm tidying up. Other than that, it sits there quietly most of the time. As for connecting everything to Wi-Fi, I still prefer using my trusty old laptop whenever possible. At least I know what I'm getting into. The idea of linking multiple appliances together seems rather complicated and unnecessary to me. Still, I suppose it might come in handy later on when I can figure out exactly how it works... maybe with a little more guidance from someone who knows their way around such contraptions!

Well, I think prioritising is all about weighing what's most important to me against my limited capacity and resources. When it comes to making decisions, I find myself leaning towards considering both short-term and long-term implications. If something affects either my health, relationships, or security, those usually take precedence. In particular, since becoming a widow six years ago, I must admit that safeguarding my financial future has become even more crucial for me. But alongside that, spending quality time with my grandkids is always near the top of my list – watching them grow up is such an incredible privilege! And of course, taking proper care of my home and personal space isn't far behind; I like being able to live comfortably while still staying grounded. It does make sense to balance these various aspects, though. For instance, ensuring I'm financially secure allows me greater freedom to engage with loved ones and pursue hobbies I truly love, which brings me joy and satisfaction. So ultimately, finding common ground between seemingly disparate priorities seems key.
